name: Devinyl
classification_notes: An all-capitals monolinear family mixing art-deco, grotesque, stencil and inline styles across eight variants; the stylistic variety and uppercase-only construction place it firmly in the display category.
summary: An all-capitals monolinear family in eight stylistic variants, ranging from art-deco inline to street-art stencil, with Latin and Cyrillic coverage.
description: Released in 2016 with Cyrillic added in 2021, Devinyl draws its uppercase from late 19th-century grotesques and its lowercase principles from humanist sans, though the family is composed entirely in capitals. Eight styles share the same underlying skeleton while offering distinct treatments: Regular, Eclipse, Fold, Incise, Inline, Line, Stencil and Vinyl. The monolinear construction and stylistic breadth make it well-suited to poster and magazine work where visual contrast between variants is useful.
foundry_blurb: Nootype describe Devinyl as a monolinear typeface family that mixes different styles, composed entirely in capitals. The uppercase draws on old grotesques from the late 19th century, while the lowercase references humanist sans. Eight styles are included, from the art-deco-inspired Line to the Stencil, which they associate with street art. All styles share the same base, and the family supports an extensive range of Latin and Cyrillic languages, including alternates for Bulgarian, Serbian and Macedonian.

name: Exergue
classification_notes: A serif with concave stems and diamond-shaped dots, giving it a distinctive vibrating optical quality that sits between classical serif structure and expressive display design.
summary: A serif with concave stems that give composed text a trembling, almost shimmering visual quality, available in five weights with italics across Latin and Cyrillic.
description: The concave stems and diamond-shaped dots are the defining structural choices here, creating a vibrating optical effect that reads well at headline sizes. Five weights run from Light to Black, each with a corresponding italic, and the OpenType feature set covers small caps, multiple figure styles, fractions and ligatures. Language support is broad, covering Latin across dozens of languages and Cyrillic with alternates for Bulgarian, Serbian and Macedonian.
foundry_blurb: Nootype describe Exergue as a dynamic serif with concave stems that give text a tremulous, almost shining appearance. Diamond-shaped dots appear throughout, and the family spans five weights from Light to Black with matching italics. The foundry notes it is suited to headlines while the range of styles also provides flexibility for text use, with OpenType features including small caps, tabular and proportional figures, fractions and ligatures, and support for Latin and Cyrillic scripts.

name: Radikal
classification_notes: Described explicitly as geometric, with a focus on purity of form. The clean, constructed letterforms and wide weight range are characteristic of the geometric sans tradition.
summary: A geometric sans in seven weights with matching italics, covering Latin and Cyrillic for editorial and display use.
description: Released in 2013 with Cyrillic added in 2021, this geometric sans spans UltraThin to Black with corresponding italics, giving fourteen styles in total. The design is oriented around formal purity, with each font carrying a full set of OpenType features including fractions, superscript, tabular figures and ordinals. Cyrillic coverage includes alternate shapes for Bulgarian, Serbian and Macedonian, broadening its reach across Eastern European markets.
foundry_blurb: Nootype describe Radikal as a geometric font dedicated to the research of purity, released in October 2013 with Cyrillic support added in 2021. The family spans seven weights from UltraThin to Black with matching italics, and is positioned as suitable for both magazines and posters, offering flexibility across text and title use.

name: Strikt Sans
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a grotesque family with low contrast and exaggerated curves; closed apertures on G, S and C reinforce the grotesque classification.
summary: A grotesque sans with exaggerated closed curves and a sharply angled 30° italic, released by Nootype in 2020.
description: Strikt Sans takes the grotesque tradition and pushes it toward tighter, more enclosed letterforms: G, S and C are fully closed across all styles, giving the family a distinctive density. The italic cuts in at an accentuated 30° angle, producing a stressed, energetic feel rather than a polite slope. Four weights from Regular to Black each have italic companions, and the character set covers Central, Eastern and Western European languages with a full suite of OpenType figure styles and fractions.
foundry_blurb: Nootype describe Strikt Sans as a grotesque family built around exaggerated curves and low contrast, with letters such as G, S and C fully closed in every style. The italic is set at a steep 30° angle to give it a stressed and distinctive appearance. Released in November 2020, it includes extensive OpenType features — alternates, proportional and tabular figures, old-style figures, superscript, subscript, ordinals, fractions and more — and supports Central, Eastern and Western European languages.

name: Rubis
classification_notes: Rubis blends calligraphic terminal forms with the sharpness of a modern serif, placing it between transitional and modern traditions with a sharp, fluid character suited to long text.
summary: A ten-style contemporary serif from 2016, designed for long-form text with sharp terminals and fluid curves drawn from calligraphic tradition.
description: Released by Nootype in February 2016, Rubis spans five weights from Light to Black, each with a matching italic, mixing calligraphic terminal forms with the crispness of a modern serif. Regular and Medium are optimised for body text, while Light and Black work at display sizes. OpenType features include small caps, proportional and tabular figures, fractions and ligatures, with extended character support for Central, Eastern and Western European languages.
foundry_blurb: Nootype describe Rubis as a contemporary serif with a sharp aspect, designed for long running text, with a serious character that retains a certain charm. Every letter and symbol was drawn around the idea of flawless curves, visible in the fluid calligraphic terminals. The family mixes classical influence with the sharpness of a modern typeface, and is recommended as an excellent choice for editorial use.

name: Lazare Grotesk
classification_notes: Explicitly named a Grotesk by the foundry, with the high contrast in heavier weights and dynamic character typical of the grotesque tradition. The backslanted variant adds an unusual expressive dimension uncommon in classical grotesques.
summary: A 21-style grotesque with seven weights, italics and a backslanted variant, covering Latin and Cyrillic with broad OpenType support.
description: Released in December 2020 with Cyrillic added in 2021, the family spans UltraThin to Black across upright, italic and backslanted cuts — the latter a relatively rare inclusion that opens up unconventional layout options. Contrast increases notably in the Black weight, giving the heaviest styles a more expressive, almost display character. Cyrillic coverage includes alternate shapes for Bulgarian, Serbian and Macedonian, alongside an extensive Latin language list.
foundry_blurb: Nootype describe Lazare Grotesk as a dynamic and strong new Grotesk comprising 21 styles across seven weights, with both italic and backslanted variants for expressive layout possibilities. The family includes a wide range of OpenType features — alternates, old style figures, fractions, superscript and more — and supports Latin and Cyrillic scripts, with locale-specific alternates for Bulgarian, Serbian and Macedonian.

name: Primera
classification_notes: A wide, low-contrast sans with a futuristic and technological character, achieved through prominent horizontal strokes and very low stroke contrast, placing it within the geometric sans tradition.
summary: A wide, low-contrast geometric sans released in 2016, spanning seven weights from UltraThin to Black with a futuristic character defined by prominent horizontal strokes.
description: Designed with a deliberately wide stance and very low stroke contrast, Primera achieves a rough, technological feel that sets it apart from conventional geometric sans families. The Regular and Medium weights are optimised for running text at small sizes, while the full range from UltraThin to Black gives the family flexibility across display and body work. Extensive OpenType features include small caps, tabular and proportional figures, fractions and ordinals, alongside support for Central, Eastern and Western European languages.
foundry_blurb: Nootype describe Primera as a wide sans with a futuristic and technological aspect, accentuated by horizontal strokes and very low contrast, giving it a rough appearance. Released in November 2016, the Regular and Medium weights are optimised for long running texts at small sizes, while the broad weight range from UltraThin to Black provides flexibility. The family includes a comprehensive set of OpenType features and extended character support for European languages.

name: Charte Mono
classification_notes: Explicitly described as a monospace typeface with a six-weight family; the mix of radical geometric shapes and softer curves places it in a playful monospaced sans category.
summary: A six-weight monospace family that pairs sharp geometric letterforms with softer curves, covering Latin and Cyrillic scripts with Bulgarian, Serbian and Macedonian alternates.
description: The design plays on contrast between angular forms such as the M and W and rounder letters like the a, r and e, while perpendicular terminals on the f and t create additional texture within running text. Six weights run from Thin to ExtraBold, each including OpenType fractions, stylistic alternates and automatic arrows. Cyrillic coverage extends to a broad range of languages with alternate shapes for Bulgarian, Serbian and Macedonian.
foundry_blurb: Nootype describe Charte Mono as a playful monospace family that mixes radical shapes, citing the M and W, with softer curves such as the a, creating contrast in forms across the family. The perpendicular f and t produce interesting combinations alongside the rounder r and e. The six-style family includes OpenType features such as fractions, stylistic alternates and automatic arrows, and supports Latin and Cyrillic with alternate shapes for Bulgarian, Serbian and Macedonian.

name: Solanel
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a neo-grotesque, with low contrast, smooth curves and a wide weight range from UltraThin to Black, placing it firmly in the neo-grotesque tradition.
summary: A low-contrast neo-grotesque released in 2016, notable for its 'rotalic' italic that combines rotation and slant rather than simply obliquing the roman.
description: Running from UltraThin to Black across both upright and italic cuts, the family also extends into a full Condensed sub-family. The italic is a considered departure from convention: rather than a slanted roman, it blends rotation and slant to produce a livelier, more calligraphic feel. An extensive OpenType feature set includes small caps, multiple figure styles, fractions and ordinals, alongside broad coverage of Central, Eastern and Western European Latin characters.
foundry_blurb: Nootype describe Solanel as a neo-grotesque with flawless curves, smooth and low-contrasted with subtle, effortless details. They highlight the italic as a 'rotalic' construction — part rotated, part slanted — rather than a simple sloped roman, and point to the wide weight range from UltraThin to Black and a rich OpenType feature set supporting European languages.

name: Felice
classification_notes: Described as having a humanistic touch with elegant proportions, placing it within the humanist serif tradition. Italics are prominent, supporting its role as a refined text face.
summary: A ten-style humanist serif released in 2013, with italics designed as a core feature for refined text setting.
description: Released in September 2013, this elegant serif covers five weights from Light to Black, each with a matching italic. The humanist character gives warmth to body text, while the italics are treated as a primary tool for typographic refinement rather than an afterthought. Each style includes small caps, a full suite of OpenType figure sets, fractions, ordinals and multiple ligatures, with extended Latin coverage for Central, Eastern and Western European languages.
foundry_blurb: Nootype describe Felice as an elegant serif font family with a humanistic touch that lends warmth to text setting. The family spans ten styles from Light to Black with italics, and each font includes small caps, OpenType features such as proportional and tabular figures, numerators, superscripts, fractions and ligatures. The extended character set supports Central, Eastern and Western European languages.

name: Kleide
classification_notes: Described as inspired by handwriting, Kleide sits within the humanist sans tradition where calligraphic origins inform letterform construction and overall elegance.
summary: A handwriting-inspired humanist sans in six weights with italics, released by Nootype in 2014 with broad European character support.
description: Six weights from Thin to Black, each with a matching italic, give the family reasonable range for editorial and branding work. Calligraphic influence runs through the letterforms, lending a lightness suited to refined or fashion-adjacent contexts. OpenType features include fractions, ordinals, proportional and tabular figures, and a generous set of ligatures, with character coverage extending to Central, Eastern and Western European languages.
foundry_blurb: Nootype describe Kleide as an elegant font inspired by handwriting, released in July 2014. The family spans six weights from Thin to Black with corresponding italics, and includes a wide range of OpenType features covering figures, fractions, superscripts, subscripts and ligatures. Extended character sets support Central, Eastern and Western European languages.

name: Stratic Script
classification_notes: Classified as script due to its handwriting origins and connected letterforms. The near-absence of stroke contrast and the careful letter connections place it in the handwriting script subcategory rather than calligraphic or brush script.
summary: A seven-weight connected script with near-zero stroke contrast, designed for legibility in fashion and editorial contexts.
description: Released in 2016, this script family draws from handwriting while keeping stroke contrast to a minimum, giving it a cleaner, more contemporary feel than many calligraphic alternatives. Seven weights run from Hairline through to a Cut variant, positioned close together so designers can pick precisely the right heaviness for a given application. OpenType features including proportional and tabular figures and standard and discretionary ligatures support refined typesetting across fashion, publishing and poster work.
foundry_blurb: Nootype describe Stratic Script as an elegant seven-font family based on handwriting, conceived around the idea of a script with almost no contrast that remains easy to use and quite legible. Every letter connects carefully to the next across all weights, and the six closely spaced weights allow precise weight selection. Suggested uses include fashion magazines, posters and book covers.

name: Qiproko
classification_notes: Semi-modular construction with squared curves and a stencil variant place this firmly in geometric display territory, with retro-technological character rather than conventional text or sans-serif classification.
summary: A semi-modular geometric typeface with squared curves evoking CRT monitors, offered across five widths and a stencil variant referencing electronic circuitry.
description: Released in February 2018, Qiproko pairs semi-modular geometry with rounded-square curves that call back to cathode ray tube screens, giving it a retro-technological character. The family spans five widths from Condensed to Extended, each mirrored by a stencil cut whose interrupted strokes draw from the visual language of printed circuit boards. Coverage extends to Central, Eastern and Western European Latin, and OpenType features include tabular figures and capital alignment.
foundry_blurb: Nootype describe Qiproko as a semi-modular typeface with geometric shapes whose squared curves reference the form of cathode ray tube monitors, lending the characters a retro feel. The stencil variant makes a direct visual reference to electronic circuits, adding a technological dimension. The family is suitable for headlines and epigraphs but functions in text as well, with extended Latin character sets for European language support.

name: Rubiesque
classification_notes: The foundry describes Rubiesque as combining humanist and grotesque references, placing it firmly in the humanist sans tradition while drawing on grotesk structure for a distinctive hybrid character.
summary: A humanist sans with grotesque leanings, sharing its skeleton with the serif family Rubis across ten styles from Light to Black with matching italics.
description: Released in February 2016, Rubiesque is the sans companion to Rubis, the two families sharing the same underlying skeleton and weight range. The mix of humanist and grotesque references gives the text weights a warmth suited to body copy, while the italics add texture at longer settings. OpenType features include small caps, multiple figure styles, fractions, ordinals and ligatures, with character coverage extending across Central, Eastern and Western European languages.
foundry_blurb: Nootype describe Rubiesque as the sans serif counterpart to Rubis, built on the same skeleton and weights. They position it as a family combining humanist and grotesque references for a unique personality, with Light and Medium weights suited to text setting and italics providing textural interest. The ten-style family is pitched as flexible and well-suited to editorial use.
